causes: Variable is out of scope (declared in a different block)
WJ0001.explanation: The compiler cannot find a variable with this name in the current scope. This usually means the variable hasn't been declared yet, or it's out of scope.
solutions: Check the spelling of the variable name
Declare the variable before using it: let x = 42
Make sure the variable is in scope
WJ0001.title: Variable not found

WJ0002.explanation: The compiler cannot find a function with this name. This might be because the function hasn't been defined, or the module containing it hasn't been imported.
solutions: Check the spelling of the function name
Define the function before calling it
Import the module: use std::collections::HashMap
WJ0002.title: Function not found

WJ0003.explanation: The compiler expected one type but found another. In Windjammer, types must match exactly. You may need to convert between types explicitly.
solutions: Use .parse() to convert string to number: "42".parse()
Use .to_string() to convert number to string: 42.to_string()
Check the function signature for expected types
WJ0003.title: Type mismatch

causes: Trying to modify an immutable variable
WJ0004.explanation: Variables in Windjammer are immutable by default. To modify a variable, you must declare it as mutable using 'let mut'.
solutions: Declare the variable as mutable: let mut x = 42
Create a new variable instead of modifying the existing one
WJ0004.title: Cannot modify immutable variable

WJ0005.explanation: The compiler cannot find a type with this name. This might be a typo, or the type might be defined in a module that hasn't been imported.
solutions: Check the spelling of the type name
Define the type (struct, enum, etc.)
Import the module containing the type
WJ0005.title: Type not found

causes: Module not in the correct directory
WJ0006.explanation: The compiler cannot find a module with this name. Check that the module exists and is in the correct location.
solutions: Check the spelling of the module name
Create the module file
Check the module is in the correct location
WJ0006.title: Module not found

causes: Value passed to function that takes ownership
Auto-clone system couldn't insert clone automatically
WJ0007.example:
// Usually handled automatically, but if not:
let data = vec![1, 2, 3]
process(data.clone())  // Explicit clone
println!("{}", data.len())
WJ0007.explanation: This value was moved to another location and can no longer be used here. In Windjammer, most values can only be used once unless they implement Copy. However, the auto-clone system should handle most cases automatically.
solutions: Use a reference (&) instead: process(&data)
Clone explicitly: process(data.clone())
Restructure code to avoid multiple uses
WJ0007.title: Value was moved

causes: Mutable borrow while immutable borrow exists
WJ0008.explanation: You're trying to borrow a value as mutable, but it's already borrowed as immutable, or vice versa. Windjammer enforces Rust's borrowing rules to ensure memory safety.
solutions: Ensure borrows don't overlap in scope
Use only one mutable borrow at a time
Restructure code to avoid conflicting borrows
WJ0008.title: Cannot borrow as mutable

WJ0009.explanation: When creating a struct, you must provide values for all fields.
solutions: Add the missing field
Check field names match struct definition
WJ0009.title: Missing field in struct

WJ0010.explanation: Your match expression doesn't cover all possible cases. Windjammer requires all patterns to be handled for safety.
solutions: Add missing match arms
Add a wildcard pattern: _ => ...
WJ0010.title: Pattern match not exhaustive
